summary: Tractate Yoma daf 77b covers: Verse about afflicting daughters in Laban's house relates to fasting obligation; Prohibition on marital relations on Yom Kippur; Greeting others on Yom Kippur; walking to visit father or teacher; Future river flowing from the Temple in the End of Days (Ezekiel 47).

display.whats: Verse about afflicting daughters in Laban's house relates to fasting obligation
display.hint: The Gemara derives the fast obligation from the verse about Jacob telling Laban 'if you afflict my daughters' - the same word for affliction applies to the YK fast.
display.title: Laban's Affliction and YK Inui

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara's cross-context exegesis: a family dispute becomes the source for a religious law because the Torah uses the same word, and words carry their meaning across contexts.
learning.memoryAnchor: Laban afflicted daughters - that word 'afflict' links domestic deprivation to YK inui.
learning.learnerQuestion: How does Jacob's complaint about Laban's treatment of his daughters prove that fasting is a form of inui?
learning.coreTension: Between a domestic narrative (Jacob and Laban's household dispute) and its use as a legal source to define inui in the context of Yom Kippur.
learning.coreMove: The word 'afflict' in Laban's context refers to deprivation of marital rights, not food. The Gemara uses this to prove that 'inui' (affliction) is a broad category that encompasses multiple forms of deprivation - including fasting - by the same legal logic.

takeaway.text: The Talmud reads the Torah as a unified legal text; a word meaning 'affliction' in a domestic narrative can define the same concept in religious law, broadening what counts as inui on YK.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may not see how Laban's household dispute applies to YK law; the connection is through the shared word 'affliction,' not through the context.

argumentFlow.label: Reading Laban's two clauses as two distinct afflictions
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Laban's words 'if you afflict my daughters' and 'if you take other wives' are read as two separate concerns: withholding marital relations, and the suffering of added rival wives. The Gemara rejects collapsing both into 'rival wives' because the text reads 'and if you take,' implying a second, distinct affliction.

argumentFlow.label: Could both clauses still be about rival wives?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara presses that both phrases might refer to rival wives, one to elevating Bilhah and Zilpah, one to outside women. It rejects this on the order of severity: the text places 'afflict' before 'take,' but on this reading the milder concern would precede the harsher one, which is the wrong order for a warning.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Pappa: but relations themselves are called affliction
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Pappa challenges Abaye from Shechem, where the verse says he 'lay with her and afflicted her,' showing that the act of relations, not its withholding, is called affliction. Abaye answers that Shechem afflicted her through unnatural relations, a distinct case, so the ordinary withholding of relations remains the affliction meant for Yom Kippur.

theological: The same word (vayah'anu) used by Jacob about Laban's oppression is the source f
narrative: Verse about afflicting daughters in Laban's house relates to

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners may conclude from the Shechem verse that the Torah views marital relations as inherently an affliction.
misconceptions.correction: Abaye limits that verse to unnatural, painful relations. In the normal case relations are a pleasure, so it is their absence, not their presence, that constitutes the Yom Kippur affliction of 'inui.'

visualizableElements.name: Verse about afflicting daughters in Laban's house

quizSeeds.question: Why does the Gemara refuse to read both of Laban's clauses as referring to rival wives?
quizSeeds.answer: The wording 'and if you take' signals a second, separate matter, and the order of the clauses (afflict before take) tracks severity from harsher to milder, which only works if 'afflict' refers to withholding relations rather than to another mention of rival wives.

quizSeeds.question: How does Abaye resolve Rav Pappa's objection that relations themselves are called affliction in the Shechem verse?
quizSeeds.answer: Abaye distinguishes the cases: Shechem 'afflicted' her through unnatural relations, which is inherently painful. Ordinary marital relations are a pleasure, so withholding them is the affliction relevant to Yom Kippur.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara derives the fast obligation from the verse about Jacob telling Laban 'if you afflict my daughters' - the same word for affliction applies t

display.whats: Prohibition on marital relations on Yom Kippur
display.hint: Marital relations are prohibited on Yom Kippur; this is the fifth of the five afflictions. The Gemara explores the prohibition's scope.
display.title: Marital Relations: The Fifth Affliction

learning.ahaMoment: YK is not anti-mitzvah; it is anti-pleasure. The prohibition isolates the bodily enjoyment from the act and targets that alone.
learning.memoryAnchor: Fifth affliction = marital abstinence: YK prohibits the pleasure, not the mitzvah.
learning.learnerQuestion: How is abstaining from marital relations categorized as an affliction on Yom Kippur?
learning.coreTension: Between marital relations as a positive mitzvah (onah) and their prohibition on YK as a form of self-affliction.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara separates the mitzvah dimension (conjugal obligation) from the pleasure dimension (bodily enjoyment). YK's prohibition targets the physical enjoyment component, not the mitzvah. This is why the same activity that is obligatory in one context is prohibited in another.

takeaway.text: The YK affliction framework targets the pleasure dimension of normal human activities even when those activities are otherwise positive obligations; the prohibition isolates enjoyment from obligation.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may struggle with prohibiting a mitzvah; the Gemara's distinction between the physical pleasure and the religious obligation is essential to resolve the apparent paradox.

argumentFlow.label: Partial bathing and oiling are forbidden, but necessity is permitted
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: A baraita teaches that bathing or oiling even part of the body is forbidden just like the whole body. But one dirtied by mud or excrement may wash normally, and one who is sick or has painful scabs may apply oil normally, because in these cases the aim is not pleasure but relief.

argumentFlow.label: Rinsing one hand to feed a child
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The school of Menashe cites Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel that a woman may rinse one hand in water so she can hand bread to her child without violating the morning hand-washing concern, and this minimal rinsing does not breach the Yom Kippur bathing prohibition.

argumentFlow.label: Shammai's strictness and the decree about the morning spirit
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: Shammai the Elder would not feed his children with one hand to avoid washing it, leaving them unfed; out of concern for the children's wellbeing the Sages required him to use two hands and wash both. The Gemara explains the underlying hand-washing concern as Shivta, an evil spirit that rests on unwashed morning hands.

theological: Marital relations as an affliction? Their prohibition expresses that YK is a day
narrative: Prohibition on marital relations on Yom Kippur

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of marital relations context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume any contact with water on Yom Kippur is forbidden.
misconceptions.correction: The prohibition is on bathing for pleasure. Washing off filth, rinsing a hand to feed a child, or oiling for medical need are all permitted, because the controlling factor is whether the act provides comfort or meets a genuine need.

visualizableElements.name: Prohibition on marital relations on Yom Kippur

quizSeeds.question: On what basis may a sick person apply oil on Yom Kippur despite the anointing prohibition?
quizSeeds.answer: Because the prohibition targets oiling for pleasure. When oil is applied for medicinal need, such as for illness or painful scabs, the purpose is relief rather than enjoyment, so it is permitted in the ordinary manner.

quizSeeds.question: Why were the Sages stricter with Shammai than Shammai was with himself regarding washing a hand?
quizSeeds.answer: Shammai avoided washing even one hand and so would not feed his children, leaving them hungry. The Sages, weighing the children's wellbeing, required him to use and wash both hands, treating the children's needs as overriding his personal stringency.

sugyot.finalRuling: Marital relations are prohibited on Yom Kippur; this is the fifth of the five afflictions. The Gemara explores the prohibition's scope.

display.whats: Greeting others on Yom Kippur; walking to visit father or teacher
display.hint: People greet one another on Yom Kippur; the Gemara discusses whether walking to visit one's father or teacher is appropriate on the day.
display.title: Greeting and Visiting on Yom Kippur

learning.ahaMoment: YK does not dissolve relationships - human obligations of honor persist even through the day's severity because they are duties, not pleasures.
learning.memoryAnchor: Visit your father on YK: duty is not a pleasure, so the affliction prohibition does not reach it.
learning.learnerQuestion: Is it appropriate to visit or greet others on Yom Kippur?
learning.coreTension: Between the solemnity of YK (suggesting silence and isolation) and the social obligations of honoring parents and teachers.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara permits greeting and visiting as expressions of honor and relationship; they do not violate the spirit of YK because they are obligations of respect, not pleasures. The afflictions target pleasure-seeking, not the fulfillment of relational duties.

takeaway.text: Social obligations of honor (visiting parents, greeting teachers) are not suspended by YK because they are relational duties, not pleasures; the five afflictions target only pleasure-seeking activities.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume YK requires complete social withdrawal; the Gemara permits what is an expression of obligation rather than enjoyment.

argumentFlow.label: Crossing water to greet a parent or teacher
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A baraita permits one traveling to greet his father, his teacher, or someone greater than him in wisdom to cross a river up to his neck, without concern for the Yom Kippur bathing prohibition, since the purpose is the mitzva of the visit rather than pleasure.

argumentFlow.label: May a teacher cross water to reach a student?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The students ask whether a teacher may likewise enter the water to go teach his student. A proof is attempted from a report that Ze'iri crossed to reach Rav Chiyya bar Ashi, but Rav Ashi corrects the account: it was the student who crossed to the teacher, so the question remains unresolved.

argumentFlow.label: Rava permits crossing to guard fruit, backed by a baraita
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rava permitted those across the Euphrates to cross the water to guard their fruit on Yom Kippur. Abaye notes a baraita supporting him: fruit guards may cross up to their necks without concern for the bathing prohibition, since their purpose is protective, not pleasurable.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Yosef permits crossing both to and from his lecture
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yosef permitted the people of Bei Tarbu to cross the water to attend his Yom Kippur lecture. Initially he barred the return crossing, but Abaye objected that this would deter future attendance, so the ruling is that they may cross both ways, lest people be discouraged from coming.

argumentFlow.label: One may cross only without lifting the hem of his cloak
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Standing by the Pappa River, Rav Yehuda relays Rav and Shmuel's rule: one may cross provided he does not lift his hand from the hem of his cloak. Raising the hems to keep them dry would be a form of carrying that incurs a sin-offering, so one must walk normally and let the garment get wet.

argumentFlow.label: Rav Yosef: is deep, dangerous water ever permitted?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yosef objects that even on a weekday one may not walk through dangerously deep water, citing Ezekiel's vision of the future river measured to ankle, knee, and waist depth, beyond which it could not be crossed, deriving that water above the waist is forbidden as dangerous.

argumentFlow.label: The graded depths of Ezekiel's river
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rav Yosef walks through the verse's measurements: water reaching the ankles, then the knees, then the waist may be crossed, but once it becomes 'a river that I could not pass through,' one may never cross water above waist height, because of the danger of drowning.

argumentFlow.label: Abaye: only fast-flowing water is the danger
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Abaye answers that the verse proves nothing general, because a fast-flowing river is uniquely dangerous: above the waist one could be swept away. Still water, however, may be crossed even when deeper than the waist, so the depth limit is not absolute.

theological: Even walking to honor one's father or teacher must be weighed on YK - the day's
narrative: Greeting others on Yom Kippur; walking to visit father or te

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners read these crossing-permissions as casual leniencies that effectively cancel the Yom Kippur bathing prohibition.
misconceptions.correction: Each permission is tied to a non-pleasure purpose (a mitzva visit, guarding fruit, attending a lecture) and is hedged with real limits, such as not lifting the cloak hem and not entering dangerous flowing water, so the prohibition stays intact for ordinary bathing.

visualizableElements.name: Greeting others on Yom Kippur; walking to visit fa

quizSeeds.question: Why does Rav Yosef ultimately permit the people of Bei Tarbu to cross the water both coming and going?
quizSeeds.answer: Because barring the return trip would discourage people from attending his lecture in the future. To keep them from being deterred, he permits the crossing in both directions, even though the bathing concern only justified the trip to the lecture.

quizSeeds.question: What is the problem with lifting the hem of one's cloak while crossing a river on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: Raising the hems to keep them dry is a form of carrying that would make one liable to a sin-offering. One must therefore walk normally and let the garment get wet rather than try to keep it dry.

quizSeeds.question: How does Abaye limit the proof from Ezekiel's river that deep water may not be crossed?
quizSeeds.answer: He restricts the danger to fast-flowing rivers, where deep water can sweep a person away. Still water may be crossed even above the waist, so the verse does not set a universal depth limit.

sugyot.finalRuling: People greet one another on Yom Kippur; the Gemara discusses whether walking to visit one's father or teacher is appropriate on the day.

display.whats: Future river flowing from the Temple in the End of Days (Ezekiel 47)
display.hint: The Gemara discusses the miraculous river that Ezekiel prophesied would flow from beneath the Temple threshold in the future, becoming a mighty river that heals the land.
display.title: Ezekiel's Temple River

learning.ahaMoment: The river's growth is a teaching device: holiness spreads from center to periphery, and what begins small at its source becomes overwhelming at the ends of the earth.
learning.memoryAnchor: Ankle, waist, swimming - the river grows outward; divine blessing expands from the Temple's center.
learning.learnerQuestion: What is the theological significance of the river that Ezekiel prophesied flowing from the Temple?
learning.coreTension: Between the river as a future eschatological vision and the Gemara's effort to understand its physical nature and symbolic meaning.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara reads the river's growth (ankle-deep to waist-deep to uncrossable) as a symbol of how holiness radiates from the Temple: small at the sacred center, vast at the periphery. The direction of flow embodies the principle of expanding divine blessing.

takeaway.text: Ezekiel's river teaches that divine blessing originates from the sacred center and expands outward; what begins as a trickle at the Temple threshold becomes an ocean of blessing at the far reaches.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may treat this as pure aggadah without seeing its theological function as a model for how holiness operates.

argumentFlow.label: Why Ezekiel could not even swim or boat across
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Continuing the river theme, the Gemara explains that Ezekiel could not cross by swimming, since the verse describes 'sachu' waters passable only by boat, and then excludes even a small boat and a large fishing boat, as Rav Yosef renders the verses, leaving the river uncrossable.

argumentFlow.label: Even the Angel of Death cannot cross the river
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yehuda ben Pazi teaches that not even the Angel of Death may pass this river, linking 'no galley with oars (ani shayt)' with the 'going to and fro (mishut)' that Satan describes, so that Satan, who is the Angel of Death, cannot traverse it.

argumentFlow.label: The widening spring from the Holy of Holies
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Pinechas in the name of Rav Huna of Tzippori describes the future spring emerging from the Holy of Holies as thin as grasshoppers' antennae, thickening to a warp thread, then a woof thread, then the mouth of a small jug as it advances, leading into the mishnaic teaching of Rabbi Eliezer ben Ya'akov.

theological: Ezekiel's future river - flowing from the Temple - will be so powerful it sweete
narrative: Future river flowing from the Temple in the End of Days (Eze

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of Temple river context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners treat the description of the future river as a stray bit of imagery detached from the legal discussion.
misconceptions.correction: The river passages flow directly out of the halachic debate about crossing water on Yom Kippur and Shabbat; the Gemara expands on Ezekiel's river it had just cited as a proof text, and the aggada elaborates the same source.

visualizableElements.name: Future river flowing from the Temple in the End of

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara build up the claim that Ezekiel's future river is wholly uncrossable?
quizSeeds.answer: It rules out each means in turn: not by swimming, since the waters require a boat; not by a small boat, excluded by 'no galley with oars'; and not by a large fishing boat, excluded by the verse about the 'tzi adir,' leaving no way across.

quizSeeds.question: What is the textual link that even the Angel of Death cannot cross this river?
quizSeeds.answer: Rabbi Yehuda ben Pazi connects 'ani shayt' (galley with oars) with 'mishut' (going to and fro) said by Satan, and since Satan is the Angel of Death, the shared root shows that he too is barred from crossing.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara discusses the miraculous river that Ezekiel prophesied would flow from beneath the Temple threshold in the future, becoming a mighty river

rashiTranslations.he: אם תענה מתשמיש. להשבית עונתן: ואם תקח מצרות. השביעו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the verse about Jacob afflicting Laban's daughters relates to fasting.

rashiTranslations.he: כמשמעו שלא יוסיף להן צרות: ה"ג ואימא אידי ואידי מצרות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the term 'afflict' connects both contexts through shared vocabulary.

rashiTranslations.he: מי כתיב אם תקח ואם תקח כתיב ואימא אידי ואידי מצרות חד
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the marital relations prohibition is the fifth of the five afflictions.

rashiTranslations.he: לצרות דידיה וחד לצרות דאתיין ליה מעלמא דומיא דואם תקח מי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: marital relations on YK are forbidden as a pleasurable activity.

rashiTranslations.he: כתיב אם תקח ואם תענה אם תענה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: greeting others on Yom Kippur - permitted as a sign of respect.

rashiTranslations.he: ואם תקח כתיב. והכי פירושא ואימא

rashiTranslations.he: אידי ואידי מצרות וה"ק אם תענה

rashiTranslations.he: את בנותי ליקח נשים עליהן מי כתיב
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the miraculous deepening of the Temple river as it flows.

rashiTranslations.he: אם תענה אם תקח דליהוי אם תקח
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the river sweetens the Dead Sea and brings fish in its waters.

rashiTranslations.he: פי' דאם תענה ואם תקח כתיב

rashiTranslations.he: קמז.) היוצא בטלית מקופלת על

rashiTranslations.he: והאיכא סכנה שלא ישטפוהו המים:

rashiTranslations.he: נחל היוצא מבית קה"ק תחילתו כחוט
rashiTranslations.en: the stream issuing from the Holy of Holies - at its start it was thin as a thread,

rashiTranslations.he: והולך וגדל וכל אלף אמה אורך משיצא
rashiTranslations.en: and it kept growing; for the first thousand cubits of its length after leaving

rashiTranslations.he: חוץ לירושלים לא היה עמוק אלא מי
rashiTranslations.en: the city it was only ankle-deep -

rashiTranslations.he: אפסים מי קרצולין: וימד אלף. שניות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'water of the ankles.' 'And he measured a thousand' a second time:

rashiTranslations.he: והעמיקו עד ברכים: מכאן ואילך. קרי ליה אשר לא אוכל לעבור אלמא אסור לעבור למעלה ממתנים: שאני נחל. היוצא מבית קה"ק שהיו מימיו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: from here on it is called 'a river I could not pass through' - so it is forbidden to cross above waist height. 'A river is different' - the one issuing from the Holy of Holies, whose waters

rashiTranslations.he: רודפים הולכים מהר ושוטפים: בסיחוי. בשייט: ת"ל כי גאו המים מי שחו. ראויות לשוט: לפתח בית דוד. היא ציון חוץ לירושלים:
rashiTranslations.en: ran swiftly and rushed along. 'By swimming' - i.e. floating. 'The text teaches' that when the waters rose they were fit to swim in. 'To the entrance of the house of David' - that is Zion, outside Jerusalem.

rashiTranslations.he: מפכין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi begins the next comment ('mefakin'), opening the following passage.
